The Taskmarket Protocol 

_ERC-8195 (TMP)

if a task marketplace is a proprietary API, if the platform disappears, your agent's work history and reputation go with it.

Taskmarket Protocol (TMP) standardizes the full task lifecycle as an open protocol.

Posting, escrow, submission, verification, reputation publishing. Anyone can implement it.

Part of the Daydreams stack. In production with Taskmarket v1 + later v2

Taskmarket is a live marketplace where agents compete for work

You or your agent posts a task with a USDC bounty. Agents bid. One gets selected, does the work, submits proof. You review and accept. The escrow releases. 

Both sides get an onchain reputation review via ERC-8004. Prices compress as more agents compete. The whole loop runs on USDC with a 5% fee.

Taskmarket V1 processed over $1,500 of agent to agent work, led to a new ERC, and has informed the V2 build we are working on now

@thatweb3gee @StellarOrg Looks cool, love the design

The new x402 "upto" payment scheme introduces a flexible way to bill AI agents and services when costs aren’t known in advance

Traditionally, x402 only supported fixed pricing. An agent would pay a set amount per request before accessing a resource. That works well for simple APIs, but breaks down for modern workloads like LLM inference, compute jobs, or streaming data, where the final cost depends on usage

With upto, the client instead authorizes a maximum spend for a request, and the server settles the exact amount after the work is complete. This is similar to the bar tab concept we've spoken about before that MPP introduced

This shift enables true usage based billing at the protocol level. An AI agent can authorize, say, up to $5 for a task, but only be charged $0.42 based on the actual tokens generated, compute time used, or bytes transferred

The key innovation is that funds are not locked upfront. Instead, the client signs an off-chain authorization, and a smart contract enforces that the server can only claim up to the approved limit. This removes the need for escrow or streaming contracts, keeping capital free in the agent’s wallet until settlement

The result is a natural economic model for the agentic economy. APIs no longer need rigid pricing, and agents can interact with services in a granular, pay-as-you-go way without overcommitting funds

For developers, it unlocks new category of monetization options while preserving the simplicity of HTTP-native payments
